original_abstract: We introduce a self-propelled colloidal hematite docker that can be steered to a small particle cargo many times its size, dock, transport the cargo to a remote location, and then release it. The self-propulsion and docking are reversible and activated by visible light. The docker can be steered either by a weak uniform magnetic field or by nanoscale tracks in a textured substrate. The light-activated motion and docking originate from osmotic/phoretic particle transport in a concentration gradient of fuel, hydrogen peroxide, induced by the photocatalytic activity of the hematite. The docking mechanism is versatile and can be applied to various materials and shapes. The hematite dockers are simple single-component particles and are synthesized in bulk quantities. This system opens up new possibilities for designing complex micrometer-size factories as well as new biomimetic systems.
humanized_abstract: We demonstrate the functionality of a self-propelled colloidal hematite docker that can remotely control guided docking with a small particle cargo many times larger than itself, pick up the cargo, remotely transport it to a release site and then release it, in multiple cycles. The self-propelled motion and docking are reversible and light activated. The hematite docker can be steered either in a weak uniform magnetic field or in nanoscale tracks fabricated as nanotrails in a textured substrate surface. The light-activated motion and docking is attributed to the osmotic/phoretic motion in a gradient of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) fuel that is generated through the photocatalytic action of hematite. The docking mechanism is highly versatile for a variety of cargo materials and shapes. Notably, the hematite-based dockers are merely single-component nanoparticles that can be synthesized in bulk amounts. The overall concept presented here is highly innovative for achieving micrometer-scale remote-controlled factories, while simultaneously opening avenues for the realization of novel bio-inspired robots for multiple tasks.

The docking mechanism is highly versatile for a variety of cargo materials and shapes. The overall concept presented here is highly innovative for achieving micrometer-scale remote-controlled factories, while simultaneously opening avenues for the realization of novel bio-inspired robots for multiple tasks.
